paragraphs.text:
Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

## NUMERIC ENCODING OPTIONS WITH AUTOMUNGE

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

Mainstream practice in machine learning with tabular data may take for granted that any feature engineering beyond scaling for numeric sets is superfluous in context of deep neural networks. This paper will offer arguments for potential benefits of extended encodings of numeric streams in deep learning by way of a survey of options for numeric transformations as available in the Automunge open source python library platform for tabular data pipelines, where transformations may be applied to distinct columns in “family tree” sets with generations and branches of derivations. Automunge transformation options include normalization, binning, noise injection, derivatives, and more. The aggregation of these methods into family tree sets of transformations are demonstrated for use to present numeric features to machine learning in multiple configurations of varying information content, as may be applied to encode numeric sets of unknown interpretation. Experiments demonstrate the realization of a novel generalized solution to data augmentation by noise injection for tabular learning, as may materially benefit model performance in applications with underserved training data.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Of the various modalities of machine learning application (e.g. images, language, audio, etc.) tabular data, aka structured data, as may comprise tables of feature set columns and collected sample rows, in my experience does not command as much attention from the research community, for which I speculate may be partly attributed to the general non-uniformity across manifestations precluding the conventions of most other modalities for representative benchmarks and availability of pre-trained architectures as could be adapted with fine-tuning to practical applications. That is not to say that tabular data lacks points of uniformity across data sets, for at its core the various feature sets can at a high level be grouped into just two primary types: numeric and categoric. It was the focus of a recent paper by this author (Author, 2020) to explore methods of preparing categoric sets for machine learning as are available in the Automunge open source python library platform for tabular data pipelines. This paper will give similar treatment for methods to prepare numeric feature sets for machine learning.

Of course it would be an to characterize “numeric feature sets” as a descriptor alone to represent the wide amount of diversity as may be found between different such instances. Numeric could be referring to integers, floats, or combinations thereof. The set of entries could be bounded, the potential range of entries could be bounded on the left, right, or both sides, the distribution of values could be thin or fat tailed, single or multi-modal. The order of samples could be independent or sequential. In some cases the values could themselves be an encoded representation of a categoric feature.

Beyond the potential diversity found within our numeric features, another source of diversity could be considered based on relationships between multiple feature sets. For example one feature could be independent of the others, could contain full or partial redundancy with one or more other variables by correlation, or in the case of sequential data there could even be causal relationships between variables across time steps.

The primary focus of transformations to be discussed in this paper will not take into account variable interdependencies, and will instead operate under the assumption that the training operation of a downstream learning algorithm may be more suitable for the efficient interpretation of such interdependencies, as the convention for Automunge is that data transformations (and in some cases

1

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

sets of transformations) are to be directed for application to a distinct feature set as input. In many cases the basis for these transformations will be properties derived from the received feature in a designated “train” set (as may be passed to the automunge(.) function) for subsequent application on a consistent basis to a designated “test” set (as may be passed to the postmunge(.) function).

## 2 NORMALIZATIONS

A common practice for preprocessing numeric feature sets for the application of neural networks is to apply a normalization operation in which received values are centered and scaled based on properties of the data. By conversion to comparable scale between features, backpropagation may have an easier time navigating the fitness landscape rather than overweighting to higher magnitude inputs (Ng, 2011). Table 1 surveys a few normalization operations as available in Automunge.

Table 1: Normalizations

|**Type**|**ID**|**Formula**|**Scaling**|
|---|---|---|---|
|z-score|‘nmbr’|(_xi −µ_)_/σ_|scaled to sigma 1 and mu 0|
|min-max|‘mnmx’|(_xi −min_)_/_(_max −min_)|scaled to unit interval|
|mean|‘mean’|(_xi −mean_)_/_(_max −min_)|scaled and centered to mean|
|MAD|‘MAD3’|(_xi −max_)_/_(_MAD_)|scaled by median absolute deviation|
|lognorm|‘lgnm’|ln(_xi_)_!_(_xi −µ_)_/σ_|log-normal scaled to Gaussian|

Upon inspection a few points of differentiation become evident. The choice of denominator can be material to the result, for while both (max - min) and standard deviation can have the result of shrinking or enlarging the values to fall within a more uniform range, the (max - min) variety has more of a known returned range for the output that is independent of the feature set distribution properties, thus allowing us to ensure all of the min-max returned values are non-negative for instance, as may be a pre-requisite for some kinds of algorithms. Of course this known range of output relies on the assumption that the range of values in subsequent test sets will correspond to the train set properties that serve as a basis - to allow a user to prevent these type of outliers from interfering with downstream applications, Automunge allows a user to pass parameters to the transformation functions, such as to activate floors or caps on the returned range.

An easy to overlook outcome of the shifting and/or centering of the returned range by way of the subtraction operation in the numerator is a loss of the original zero point, as for example with z-score normalization the returned zero point is shifted to coincide with the original mean. It is the opinion of this author that such re-centering of the data may not always be a trivial trade-off. Consider the special properties of the number 0 in mathematics, including multiplicative properties at/above/below. By shifting the original zero point we are presenting a (small) obstacle to the training operation in order to relearn this point. Perhaps more importantly, further trade-offs include the interpretability of the returned data.

Automunge thus offers a novel form of normalization, available in our library as ‘retn’ (standing for “retain”), that bases the formula applied to scale data on the range of values found within the train set, with the result of scaling the data within a similar range as some of those demonstrated above while also retaining the zero point and thus the +/- sign of all received data.

Table 2: Retain Normalization (‘retn’)

|**Min**|**Max**|**Formula**|**Returned Min**|**Returned Max**|
|---|---|---|---|---|
|__0|_≥_0|_xi/_(_max −min_)|_min/_(_max −min_)|_max/_(_max −min_)|
|_>_0|_>_0|(_xi −min_)_/_(_max −min_)|0|1|
|_<_0|_<_0|(_xi −max_)_/_(_max −min_)|-1|0|

2

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

## 3 TRANSFORMATIONS

In many cases the application of a normalization procedure may be preceded by one or more types of data transformations applied to the received numeric set. Examples of data transformations could include basic mathematic operators like + - * /, log transforms, raising to a power, absolute values, etc. In some cases the transformations may also be tailored to the properties of the train set, for example with a Box-Cox power law transformation (Box & Cox, 1964).

In the Automunge library, the order of such sets of transformations, as may be applied to a distinct source column, and in some cases which may include generations and branches of derivations, are specified by way of transformation category entries to a set of “family tree” primitives [Table 3] (Author, 2020) for a root transformation category, and where a transformation category entry may be associated with one or more transformation functions intended for application to corresponding train and/or test set feature columns, potentially including custom transformation functions which may be defined with minimal requirements of simple data structures. Such root categories may be pre-defined in the Automunge library of transformations or may be custom configured by a user in entries to a “transformdict” data structure.

Table 3: Family Tree Primitives

|**Primitive**|**Upstream /**
**Downstream**|**Applied to**
**Generation**|**Column Action**|**Downstream**
**Offspring**|
|---|---|---|---|---|
|parents
siblings
auntsuncles
cousins|upstream
upstream

frst|replace

replace
supplement|yes

no|
|children|downstream parents|offspring|replace|yes|
|niecesnephews|downstream siblings|offspring|supplement|yes|
|coworkers|downstream auntsuncles|offspring|replace|no|
|friends|downstream cousins|offspring|supplement|no|

The convention for transformation functions in the Automunge library is that any kind of function accepts any kind of data, and in cases where an invalid entry is returned, for example when dividing by zero or taking a square root of a negative number, such entry may serve as a target for missing data infill, with infill methods that may be applied to a column from a library of infill options - including “ML infill” in which random forest models (Breiman, 2001) are used to predict infill based on properties of the train set. To facilitate the application of infill, transformation categories used as root categories are specified with a classification for the types of data that will be considered valid input, as for example may be non-negative numeric, non-zero numeric, integer numeric, etc. These “NArowtype” classifications are populated in the same “processdict” data structure used to assign transformation functions to a transformation category.

## 4 BINS AND GRAININGS

In most cases the transformations considered in the preceding section maintained full information retention of the received data, such that with returned sets the form of the input data can be recovered with an inversion operation (as is available in the Automunge library). For binning transformations, there may instead be a type of coarse graining of the feature set to aggregate buckets of entries into a categoric representation. Automunge offers a wide range of options for numeric binning [Table 4]. Bins may be aggregated to either supplement or replace received numeric sets.

For each binning operation, transformation category options are available to return the categoric encoding as a one-hot encoding, ordinal integer encoding, or binary encoding in which distinct categories may be represented by multiple simultaneous activations. This is partly motivated by different conventions of various libraries for accepting input to an entity embedding layer (Guo & Berkhahn, 2016) as may be applied to the returned categoric encoding in a downstream training operation.

3

Table 4: Binning Transformation Category Options

|**Transform**|**One-Hot**|**Ordinal**|**Binary**|**Parameters**|
|---|---|---|---|---|
|Number of standard deviations from the mean|‘bins’|‘bsor’|‘bsbn’|‘bincount’|
|Powers of ten|‘pwrs’|‘pwor’|‘pwbn’|-|
|Powers of ten (with support for negative)|‘pwr2’|‘por2’|‘por3’|-|
|Fixed width bins|‘bnwd’|‘bnwo’|‘bnwb’|‘width’|
|Equal population bins
User specifed bins (frst/last unconstrained)

‘bkt1’

## 5 NOISE INJECTION

For most cases in the Automunge library, transformations applied to a train set feature set are applied to the corresponding test set feature set using the same basis, such that if the same data is received for both train and test sets, the same form will be returned (a useful point for validations). The noise injection options are a little different in that such injections may be intended just for the train data but not the corresponding test data.

et al., 2006). Other potential uses of noise injections could be to perturb the model training, facilitating diversity between models as may be beneficial in the aggregation of ensembles (Dietterich, 2000) or as a source of training data augmentation (Perez & Wang, 2017). The experiments detailed below will suggest a material model performance benefit from data augmentation by noise injection in cases of underserved training data, which we believe is a novel innovation for tabular learning.

The options available for noise injection are generally derived by way of aggregations of transformation category entries to family tree primitives, as noise injection may be applied downstream to a normalization or categoric encoding. (The convention for transformation functions is that they receive input of a single target column, so transformations performed downstream of a categoric encoding should be fed an ordinal input.) The library includes distinct noise injection family tree aggregations tailored to operation of several different types of received normalizations, as may rely on a known range or scale of input, or applied preceding different types of categoric encodings.

Table 5: Numeric Noise Injections

|||Table 5: Numeric Noise Injections|||
|---|---|---|---|---|
|**Root**
**Category**|**Normalization**|**Noise Type**|**Parameters**||
|‘DPnb’
‘DPmm’
‘DPrt’|‘nmbr’
‘mnmx’
‘retn’|Gaussian w/ Bernoulli ratio
scaled Gaussian w/ Bernoulli ratio
scaled Gaussian w/ Bernoulli ratio|‘mu’ / ‘sigma’ / ‘fip
‘mu’ / ‘sigma’ / ‘fip
‘mu’ / ‘sigma’ / ‘fip|~~p~~rob’
~~p~~rob’
~~p~~rob’|

Table 6: Categoric Noise Injections

|**Root**
**Category**|**Encoding**|**Noise Type**|**Parameters**|
|---|---|---|---|
|‘DPbn’
‘DPod’
‘DPoh’
‘DP10’|‘bnry’ (boolean)
‘ord3’ (ordinal)
‘onht’ (one-hot)

Numeric noise injections [Table 5, Fig 1] are derived from a Gaussian source with configurable parameters. For noise intended to sets with a fixed range of values such as DPmm, although the

4

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

Figure 1: ‘DPmm’ and ‘DPrt’ family trees

Figure 2: ‘DP10’ family trees

noise source as implemented is Gaussian, the application is capped from extreme outliers at half of range (e.g. +/- 0.5) and based on whether an input entry is above or below the midpoint, positive or negative noise respectively is scaled to ensure maintained original range in returned data based on values of input entry. Parameters are also accepted to indicate what ratio of input will receive injection. Similar options are available for Laplace distribution noise profiles.

To clarify how family tree primitives come into play, for the ‘DPmm’ root category in Fig 1, when ‘DPmm’ is applied as a root category to a source column with header ‘column’, the upstream primitive entries are inspected, which include a parents entry of the ‘DPm2’ transformation category and a cousins entry of the ‘NArw’ transformation category. For the ‘NArw’ upstream entry to the cousins primitive, the ‘NArw’ transformation category is associated with a NArw transformation function (a function aggregating boolean activations indicating presence of infill) per the processdict entry associated with the ‘NArw’ root category, which transformation function returns the column ‘column ~~N~~ Arw’ with the suffix appender ‘ ~~N~~ Arw’ logging the transformation function applied. Because cousins is a primitive without offspring no further generations are inspected in the ‘NArw’ family tree downstream primitives. For the upstream parents primitive entry ‘DPm2’, the ‘DPm2’ processdict entry has a transformation function of mnmx (min-max scaling), which is applied and logged by the ‘ ~~m~~ nmx’ suffix appender, and since parents is a primitive with offspring the downstream primitives in the ‘DPm2’ family tree are inspected where a coworkers entry of ‘DPmm’ transformation category is found which is associated with a DPmm transformation function (noise injection corresponding to range 0-1) based on the ‘DPmm’ root category processdict entry. Because coworkers is a replacement primitive the column configuration with header ‘column ~~m~~ nmx’ is not retained for the returned set. The returned column originating from the DPmm transformation function has header logging the two applied transformation functions as ‘column ~~m~~ nmx ~~D~~ Pmm’. Please note that column retention, as may be impacted by the application of downstream replacement primitive entries, is signaled in these diagrams by the color coding between orange and blue, and applied transformation functions are shaded as green. Transformation category entries of primitives that are not inspected are left without shading. Also please note that the processdict entries here are an abstraction for the set of corresponding transformation functions that may be directed to train and/or test set feature sets.

5

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

The application of noise to categoric encodings [Table 6, Fig 2] is a little simpler, where a given ratio of entries in a categoric feature set are flipped to one of the other activations, between which have a uniform probability of replacement (including possibility of original entry retention). For the ‘DP10’ root category example shown in Fig 2, this is achieved by first applying an ordinal encoding by ord3 transformation function associated with a ‘DPo6’ transformation category entry to the parents upstream primitive of the ‘DP10’ root category, then a noise injection by the DPod transformation function associated with a ‘DPo3’ transformation category to the downstream children primitive associated with the ‘DPo6’ root category (applied since parents is a primitive with offspring), followed by a binary encoding by the 1010 transformation function associated with the ‘1010’ transformation category entry to the downstream coworkers primitive associated with the ‘DPo3’ root category (applied since children is a primitive with offspring). Here the intermediate stages of derivations associated with columns ‘column ~~o~~ rd3’ and ‘column ~~o~~ rd3 ~~D~~ Pod’ are not retained in the returned set since children and coworkers are replacement primitives.

## 6 SEQUENTIAL DATA

For numeric features in which the order of samples carry some e.g. for time series data, some additional options are available to extract structure from relationships between time steps (these methods may benefit from a convention that time deltas between measurements are at or nearly constant). The theory is that for sequential machine learning applications, as may make use of recurrence, convolution, or attention mechanisms, there may be benefit to supplementing feature streams with properties carried forward from prior time steps, where this type of operation may be particularly beneficial when there is some known cyclic property inherent in the application, e.g. for scheduled trading hours or quarterly reports.

Included in the Automunge library are sequential transforms to supplement sequential streams with proxies for derivatives by returning deltas between an entry and some desired time step prior by way of the ‘dxdt’ family of transforms [Fig 3]. Such application may be applied once as a proxy for velocity, and may also be run multiple times upon that output as proxies for higher order derivatives. A variant on this operation may, instead of taking point-wise deltas, return deltas between averages of sets of points, which has the effect of smoothing or de-noising the data. The outputs of these operations may each be normalized such as with the ‘retn’ normalization for sign retention.

Figure 3: ‘dxdt’ family trees

## 7 INTEGER SETS

Integer feature sets of unknown origin may present a particular challenge for automated encodings, as these may be associated with a diverse set of interpretations, and may originate from continuous variables, counters, discrete relational variables (e.g. small/medium/large), or possibly even an ordinal categoric encoding (Stevens et al., 2020). The Automunge philosophy for these kind of ambiguities is to simply redundantly encode in a manner suitable for each [Table 7, Fig 4], defering to a training operation for determining relevancy.

6

Table 7: Integer Encoding Options

|**Transform**|**Type**|**Useful For**|
|---|---|---|
|‘retn’|Retain normalization|Continuous variables|
|‘pwr2’|Order of magnitude bins|Continuous variables|
|‘ordl’|Ordinal|Ordered categoric|
|‘1010’|Binary|Discrete categoric|
|‘dxdt’|Sequential|Cumulative sequential|
|‘ord3
~~m~~nmx’|Frequency sorted ordinal
followed by min-max|Scaled metric for ranking entry frequency|

Figure 4: ‘ntgr’ family trees

## 8 EXPERIMENTS

Some experiments were run to evaluate impact of various normalizations and transformations. The Higgs data set (Baldi et al., 2014) was selected based on scale and predominantly numeric feature types. The Higgs application is tabular data for binary classification sourced from high energy physics domain to evaluate subatomic particle interactions to distinguish between background noise and traces of Higgs boson interactions. The origin paper noted some details of architectures (5 layers with 300 nodes in each) that served as basis for the experiments, and we used a 0.6M size validation set out of the 8.8M samples. A departure was made from the origin paper in use of a phased learning rate with the fastai (Howard & Gugger, 2020) “fit ~~o~~ ne ~~c~~ ycle” learner for tabular data, partly for convenience along with time and resource constraints. Since the interest was primarily to evaluate relative performance between different preprocessing methods, no significant attempt was made to train models to maximum performance or to venture beyond double descent, instead the primary tuning aspect was selecting an epoch depth beyond which additional did not have improved validation metrics. The experiments were repeated with different size subsets of the training data, including full data set, 5% samples, and 0.25% samples to represent scenarios with underserved training data. Learning rates deferred to the fastai default finder, and the evaluation metric was selected as area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (ROC AUC) to be consistent with the origin demonstration.

The experiments applied Automunge to preprocess the feature sets, in each case applying uniform transformation types between features although with basis fit to properties of each respective column, and with missing data infill by way of the Automunge adjacent cell infill option. The types of transformations were selected to demonstrate impact of a few representative variants noted in this writeup, and included a base scenario with no feature scaling applied (just raw numeric data), along with a scenario for z-score normalization, retain normalization, retain normalization supplemented by standard deviation bins, two scenarios of retain normalization with noise injection (with noise injected to all entries or to 3% of entries in each feature), and finally a data augmentation with retain normalized data supplemented by doubling the number of samples to include retain with partial

7

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

Table 8: Higgs Data Normalization Scenarios (AUC metric // compared to raw data)
noise injection. Both of the noise injections applied Gaussian noise with standard deviation of 0.03 and with scaling to maintain the fixed range of the received data.

set metrics and performance delta from the raw data scenario. The AUC metric shown compares to the origin paper’s reported 88%, which was based on a reported 200-1,000 epochs compared to this experiment’s 3-42 so there is some dissimilarity in results, which can also partly be attributed to the phased learning rate schedule applied by fastai. The AUC metrics shown are averages of repetition counts detailed in Appendix G.

The scenarios for raw data, z-score, and retain normalization achieved similar results. Part of the impact of supplementing the normalized data with standard deviation bins was a small increase of training time. As expected the noise injection had a dampening effect on the model accuracy, although the partial injection had only a small penalty in comparison to the retain normalization without injection. Data augmentation improved the results, sufficiently to draw the conclusion of material benefit for scenarios with underserved training data. We believe the realization of a novel generalized solution to data augmentation by noise injection for tabular learning is a significant finding.

The experiment with the full data set was repeated to demonstrate impact to a linear model:

Table 9:

## 9 DISCUSSION

first by scale of data available for training. When approaching big data scale input with infinite computational resources there may be less of a case to be made for much beyond basic normalized input. The nuance comes into play when we are targeting applications with real world constraints. Although deep over-parameterized models may still be applied to target applications with underrepresented data (Olson et al., 2018), such methods carry overheads. It is one of the premises of

8

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

the Automunge library that supplementing our data streams with redundant features in multiple configurations may lower the bar to efficient extraction of inter-variable relationships.

My experience is that the machine learning community has by large become somewhat dismissive of any kind of feature engineering, I expect partly owed to such works as Deep Learning (Goodfellow et al., 2016) which offers that deep learning has supplanted the need for such effort. I would offer in retort that there are different kinds of feature engineering to consider. Those methods as may apply explorations and optimizations between inter-variable relationships I agree are largely redundant of what may be efficiently derived through backpropagation and this is part of the reason why Automunge hasn’t ventured into this territory. But the types of data manipulations as may be used to supplement numeric features with multiple configurations are computationally efficient, and need not require sophisticated optimizations to apply. The primary cost of such supplements are the memory overheads as the data set size is expanded.

Further, variations on feature composition, such as by variations in information content and variations on injected noise, may serve as a useful source of model perturbations in ensemble assemblies. Noise injection may be of benefit for anonymizing sensitive data for purposes of differential privacy, and may also serve as a source of training data augmentation, similar to how for convolutional networks training data images may be duplicated with artificial variations (Perez & Wang, 2017).

I’ll close by noting an interesting paper I saw at NeurIPS last year, “SGD on Neural Networks Learn Functions of Increasing Complexity” by Nakkiran et al. (2019), in which the authors found that SGD behaves as a linear model in early epochs, and importantly that characteristics of such linear models are retained even in the later stages of training. Put differently, any steps that may be made to ensure that our models may efficiently extract properties even in early stages, before deep learning can do its magic, are not immaterial to the final performance.

malized sets with proxies for derivatives|

## ACKNOWLEDGMENTS

A thank you is owed to Alice Zheng and Amanda Casari whose 2018 book “Feature Engineering for Machine Learning” served as a helpful reference as I began to explore the practice of feature engineering. Thanks to Stack Overflow, Python, PyPI, GitHub, Colaboratory, Anaconda, and Jupyter. Special thanks to Scikit-Learn, Numpy, Scipy Stats, and Pandas.

zero_shot__gemini-3-flash-preview.label: Zero Shot (gemini-3-flash-preview)
zero_shot__gemini-3-flash-preview.overall_feedback: The paper provides a comprehensive taxonomic overview of numeric encoding strategies within the Automunge library, emphasizing the utility of redundant feature engineering and data augmentation through noise injection. While the survey of transformations is well-structured and aligns with practical needs in tabular data pipelines, the primary technical contribution—a 'novel' noise-based data augmentation for tabular data—is somewhat undermined by a lack of rigorous comparison to existing benchmarks (like SMOTE or Mixup) and an informal reporting of experimental results. There are also specific ambiguities in the definitions of proposed transformations, specifically the 'retn' normalization and the 'lognorm' formula, that require clarification.

comments.title: Mathematical Error in Lognormal Scaling Formula
comments.explanation: In Table 1, the 'lgnm' (lognorm) transformation is described with a formula involving an exclamation point operator, which appears to be a typo or an undefined notation in this context. Standard log-normalization usually involves taking the log of the data followed by z-score standardization of the log-transformed values. The notation used here is confusing and does not clearly describe a standard mathematical operation. Given the context of OCR, it might be a misread of a vertical bar or a multi-step indicator, but as written, it is mathematically ambiguous.

comments.title: Ambiguity and Edge Case Risk in 'Retain' Normalization
comments.explanation: In Table 2, the 'retn' normalization for the case where Min <= 0 and Max >= 0 is defined as xi / (max - min). While the authors' intent is to retain the zero point and sign, this specific formula does not guarantee a standard scaling range (like unit interval or unit sigma). If the range (max - min) is large but the data is heavily skewed toward small values, the returned values will be arbitrarily small. More importantly, if the input distribution is, for example, [-1, 100], the returned range is [-0.0099, 0.9901], which shifts the relative magnitude compared to other features in a way that might not be intended by a user seeking 'normalization'. The text claims it scales within a 'similar range' to other methods, but this is highly sensitive to the balance between min and max.

comments.title: Insufficient Justification for 'Novelty' Claim
comments.quote: Experiments demonstrate the realization of a novel generalized solution to data augmentation by noise injection for tabular learning
comments.explanation: The authors claim that noise injection for tabular data augmentation is a novel innovation. However, noise injection (Gaussian, salt-and-pepper) and related techniques like SMOTE (Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique) or Mixup have been applied to tabular datasets for years. While the specific implementation within the Automunge 'family tree' architecture might be unique to the platform, the concept of increasing tabular model robustness via noise is a known practice in the field. The paper lacks a literature review or comparison against these existing methods to justify the claim of novelty.

comments.title: Inconsistency in Noise Injection Range Parameters
comments.quote: the application is capped from extreme outliers at half of range (e.g. +/- 0.5)
comments.explanation: In Section 5, the text states that for the 'DPmm' (min-max) noise, the noise is capped at +/- 0.5 to maintain the 0-1 range. However, if an input value is 0.1, adding a noise value of +0.5 would result in 0.6 (within range), but adding -0.5 would result in -0.4 (outside the 0-1 range). The authors later mention that noise is scaled 'based on values of input entry' to ensure the range is maintained, but the exact mechanism of this scaling is underspecified, making it difficult to reproduce how the probability distribution of the noise changes relative to the distance from the boundaries.

comments.title: Questionable Claim regarding Linear Model performance
comments.explanation: In Table 10 (and implicitly in Section 8), the authors suggest that binning/grainings are helpful for linear models. While binning does allow linear models to capture non-linear relationships, the experiment in Table 9 shows that 'Retain with Bins' significantly outperformed the others on a linear model (0.68 vs 0.64). However, there is no discussion of the 'bincount' used or how the bins were linearized (e.g., if they were one-hot encoded, which is likely). If the one-hot encoded bins were used as features for a linear model, the model is no longer a simple linear function of the input but a piece-wise constant function. The distinction between feature expansion and 'scaling' is important here for a reader to understand why the linear model improved.

zero_shot__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Zero Shot (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
zero_shot__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: The paper presents a comprehensive exploration of numeric feature encoding options within the Automunge framework, advocating for extended preprocessing in tabular deep learning. It introduces a novel 'retain' normalization and emphasizes the utility of noise injection for data augmentation, particularly in low-data regimes. The experiments support the claim of performance benefits in underserved training scenarios. While the conceptual framework is insightful and the family tree abstraction is innovative, there are technical inconsistencies in formulas and parameter definitions that raise concerns about reproducibility and correctness. The paper would benefit from clarifying derivations and correcting evident notation and formula errors.

comments.title: Incorrect formula for 'retn' normalization in Table 2
comments.explanation: The first row of Table 2 defines the 'retn' normalization for the case where min ≤ 0 and max ≥ 0 as x_i / (max - min). However, this transformation does not center or shift the data appropriately. If min ≤ 0 and max ≥ 0, then max - min is positive, but dividing by this range alone does not preserve zero in a meaningful way. For instance, if x_i = 0, the result is 0, which is correct for sign retention, but values like x_i = min become min/(max-min), which is negative, and x_i = max become max/(max-min), which is positive but not bounded. The issue is that this formula lacks a centering term. In contrast, the second and third rows use (x_i - offset)/(max - min) with appropriate offsets. The first row appears to be missing a shift. Given that the stated intent is to retain the zero point and sign, a correct version might be (x_i - min)/(max - min) for min > 0 and (x_i - max)/(max - min) for min < 0 and max < 0, but for the mixed sign case (min ≤ 0 ≤ max), the current formula x_i / (max - min) does preserve sign and maps zero to zero, but it doesn’t control the output range meaningfully. The bigger issue is that this transformation could map values outside [-1,1] or [0,1] depending on the data, undermining the goal of normalization. The authors likely intended a different formula, such as x_i / max when min ≥ 0, but that's not stated. This is a real error because it contradicts standard practices and the other cases in the table, and it affects how the method is implemented.

comments.title: Inconsistent noise injection parameter labeling in Table 5
comments.explanation:
In Table 5, the 'Parameters' column for each row lists 'mu', 'sigma', and 'fip' followed by what appears to be 'prob' with strikethrough formatting (~~p~~rob'). This formatting suggests 'fip' is the correct parameter name and 'prob' is a correction or variant, but the strikethrough persists across all rows. Given that in Table 6 the same column uses 'fip
~~p~~rob’', it seems 'fip' stands for 'flip probability' and 'prob' is an alternative notation. However, standard terminology would use 'p' or 'prob'. The confusion arises because 'fip' is not defined in the text. Later in the paper, noise injection is described as having a 'ratio of input that will receive injection', consistent with a probability parameter. The naming 'fip' without definition is underspecified, and the strikethrough formatting appears to be a typesetting artifact (possibly from OCR or editing) that leaves the intended parameter ambiguous. While the intent (a Bernoulli flip probability) is clear from context, the notation is inconsistent and unexplained, risking misinterpretation.

comments.title: Mismatch between text and Table 1 for 'MAD' normalization
comments.explanation: The formula in Table 1 for 'MAD' normalization is (x_i − max) / MAD, where MAD typically denotes the median absolute deviation. However, this is not standard. Standard robust scaling uses (x_i − median) / MAD. Using 'max' in the numerator is highly unusual and would shift the data such that the maximum becomes zero, which distorts the distribution and does not center it at the median. The text describes the row simply as “scaled by median absolute deviation” but does not explain the numerator choice. This suggests either a formula error or an unconventional definition. The authors may have intended to center at the median, not at the maximum. Given that the MAD is a robust measure of spread, centering at the maximum makes little sense. This is a mathematical error because it contradicts standard robust normalization practices and could lead to incorrect implementations.

comments.title: Ambiguity in noise injection scaling for DPmm
comments.quote: for DPmm, although the noise source as implemented is Gaussian, the application is capped from extreme outliers at half of range (e.g. +/- 0.5) and based on whether an input entry is above or below the midpoint, positive or negative noise respectively is scaled to ensure maintained original range in returned data
comments.explanation: The text describes capping noise at +/- 0.5 for DPmm (which applies to min-max normalized data, hence range [0,1]). However, it states that the sign of the noise depends on whether the input is above or below the midpoint (0.5). If input > 0.5, only positive noise is added, which could push values above 1; if input < 0.5, only negative noise is added, pushing values below 0. But the goal is to maintain the [0,1] range. The mechanism described—scaling based on input value—remains underspecified. How is the scaling done? Is the noise truncated, clipped, or scaled proportionally? The phrase 'scaled to ensure maintained original range' is too vague. Without more detail, a reader could misinterpret this as a simple clip, but the use of scaling implies a multiplicative adjustment based on distance to boundary, which differs from common practice. This lacks sufficient justification and is underspecified, risking non-reproducible implementations.

comments.title: Overstatement of novelty in noise injection for tabular data augmentation
comments.quote: we believe is a novel innovation for tabular learning
comments.explanation: The paper claims novelty for 'a novel generalized solution to data augmentation by noise injection for tabular learning'. However, adding Gaussian noise to tabular data is a standard technique in differential privacy (e.g., Dwork et al., 2006, cited in the paper) and has been used in regularization and data augmentation before. For example, 'Stochastic Activation Pruning' or 'Dropout' can be seen as noise injection, and synthetic data generation via SMOTE or VAEs includes perturbations. While the integration into a family tree pipeline may be novel, the core idea of noise injection for augmentation is not. The experiments compare noise-injected training to a baseline but do not compare to existing augmentation techniques. The claim overstates what has been shown, especially since the partial noise injection underperforms the base method, and only the full data augmentation (duplication + noise) shows gains. The benefit may come more from sample duplication than from noise injection itself.

comments.title: Incorrect formula in Table 1 for 'lognorm' normalization
comments.explanation: The formula contains 'ln(xi)_!_', which includes a factorial symbol '!' applied to a logarithm. This is mathematically nonsensical—factorials are defined for non-negative integers, not real-valued logarithms. It is likely an OCR or typesetting error. The intended formula is probably 'ln(xi)' followed by z-score normalization, i.e., (ln(x_i) - μ_ln) / σ_ln, for log-normal data to make it Gaussian-like. The text describes it as 'log-normal scaled to Gaussian', supporting this. However, the current expression is invalid. The exclamation mark might have been meant as emphasis or a delimiter but in mathematical context it creates a serious error. Given that the rest of the table uses clean LaTeX-style notation, this stands out as a genuine formula error.

zero_shot__glm-4.6.label: Zero Shot (glm-4.6)
zero_shot__glm-4.6.overall_feedback: The paper presents a comprehensive survey of numeric encoding options for tabular data using the Automunge library, introducing novel concepts like retain normalization and noise injection for data augmentation. While the overall approach and experimental methodology are sound, there are several mathematical errors in the normalization formulas that need correction. The paper would benefit from more rigorous mathematical presentation and clearer justification of some non-standard transformations.

comments.title: Incorrect MAD normalization formula
comments.explanation: The MAD (Median Absolute Deviation) normalization formula shown as (xi - max)/MAD is incorrect. MAD is defined as the median of absolute deviations from the median, so the correct formula should be (xi - median)/MAD. The current formula uses max instead of median, which would not properly center the data and defeats the purpose of MAD normalization which is robust to outliers.

comments.title: Typo in lognorm formula
comments.explanation: The lognorm formula contains an exclamation mark '!' that appears to be an OCR error or typo. The formula should likely be either ln(xi) followed by normalization, or perhaps a composition of log transform and z-score. As written, the formula is mathematically undefined.

comments.title: Misleading zero point preservation in retain normalization
comments.explanation: For the case where min ≥ 0 and max ≥ 0, the formula xi/(max - min) does preserve the zero point (0 maps to 0), but the output range [min/(max-min), max/(max-min)] does not start at 0 unless min = 0. The table claims this retains the zero point but doesn't clarify that the output range is shifted. This could mislead readers expecting the output to start at 0.

comments.title: Non-standard mean normalization formula
comments.explanation: The mean normalization formula (xi - mean)/(max - min) is unusual. While mathematically valid, it centers by mean but scales by range rather than standard deviation. This differs from standard mean normalization practices and could confuse readers expecting either mean centering alone (xi - mean) or min-max scaling. The description 'scaled and centered to mean' is also unclear - the data is centered to mean but scaled by range.

zero_shot__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Zero Shot (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
zero_shot__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback: The paper provides a useful, high-level survey of numeric transformation methods available in the Automunge library. It effectively categorizes these transformations and provides an interesting case study on the potential benefits of noise injection for tabular data augmentation in underserved training scenarios. While the description of the library's 'family tree' architecture is helpful, the paper suffers from some inconsistent notation and minor presentation issues, particularly within the tables and the provided example figures. These issues do not invalidate the overall contribution but do require closer attention to detail in future revisions.

comments.title: Inconsistent noise injection parameters in Table 5
comments.explanation: The OCR correctly captured the strike-through formatting that appears in the PDF, which seems to reflect a layout or rendering error in the final document production where 'fip' (likely a typo for 'flip' or a parameter name) and 'prob' were intended to be separate items. Given the context of Table 6 using 'fip prob' as well, this is clearly intended as a list of parameters. The strikethrough is confusing and distracting, as it obscures the intended parameter names.

comments.title: Lack of derivation or reference for the 'lgnm' formula
comments.explanation: The formula for 'lgnm' includes an exclamation point (!) as an operator which is not standard notation in this context (it could be a typo for a division sign or separator). Furthermore, the expression following it appears to be a standard z-score normalization applied *after* the log transform, rather than a single log-normal transformation formula. The derivation for why this specific combination is referred to as 'log-normal scaled to Gaussian' is insufficient.

comments.title: Use of '1010' for binary encoding nomenclature
comments.explanation: The label '1010' is used in Table 6 to describe a binary encoding, but the notation is slightly ambiguous as to whether that is a literal key or a shorthand for the bitwise representation. Given that other labels like 'bnry' are used, '1010' seems potentially mnemonic but lacks clear definition as to whether it supports specific bit-width configurations.

comments.title: Experimental claim overstatement regarding data augmentation
comments.quote: We believe the realization of a novel generalized solution to data augmentation by noise injection for tabular learning is a significant finding.
comments.explanation: The results in Table 8 show modest improvements for the 'Retain w/ Augment' scenario. While the improvement is indeed positive, calling it a 'novel generalized solution' is an overstatement without a more thorough analysis of why the noise injection works across various datasets, rather than just the Higgs dataset provided. The current scope is limited to one dataset and specific architectures.

zero_shot__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Zero Shot (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
zero_shot__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: The paper provides a practical overview of the Automunge library's numeric preprocessing and augmentation capabilities, with a clear structure and useful demonstrations of its family-tree transformation system. However, several technical and methodological concerns weaken the scientific rigor. There are noticeable formula errors in the normalization table, underspecified details in the noise injection procedure, and a limited hyperparameter tuning strategy that may bias the comparative experiments. Additionally, the novelty claims regarding noise-based data augmentation are overstated given established practices in the field, and there is a minor inconsistency between the textual summary and the experimental results table. These issues are largely fixable and should be addressed to strengthen the paper's credibility and reproducibility.

comments.title: MAD normalization formula uses incorrect numerator
comments.explanation: In Table 1, the MAD (Median Absolute Deviation) normalization is defined with the formula `(_xi −max_)_/_(_MAD_)`. Given the method name and the scaling description 'scaled by median absolute deviation', the numerator should almost certainly use the median rather than the maximum value. Using `max` would produce a shifted and scaled metric that does not align with standard MAD normalization or the stated purpose of centering by the median. This appears to be a typographical error in the formula definition that should be corrected to `(_xi −median_)_/_(_MAD_)` or similar.

comments.title: Garbled log-normal formula in Table 1
comments.explanation: The lognorm formula in Table 1 contains an unusual `!` operator that does not correspond to standard mathematical notation for normalization transformations. It is highly likely an OCR artifact or a typesetting error that merged two separate operations (e.g., a log transform followed by a z-score or min-max scaling). Because the exact intended transformation is unclear, readers cannot reproduce this specific encoding option. The authors should clarify whether this represents `ln(xi)` scaled independently, a composite transformation, or provide the correct LaTeX formula.

comments.title: Noise injection scaling procedure is underspecified
comments.quote: based on whether an input entry is above or below the midpoint, positive or negative noise respectively is scaled to ensure maintained original range in returned data based on values of input entry.
comments.explanation: The description of the Gaussian noise injection for fixed-range data (e.g., min-max normalized) explains the intent (clipping outliers, maintaining range) but lacks the precise mathematical or algorithmic steps required to reproduce the scaling logic. Phrases like 'scaled to ensure maintained original range... based on values of input entry' are vague. A careful reader would need the exact clipping bounds, the scaling factor calculation, and how edge cases (e.g., input exactly at midpoint or bounds) are handled. While the open-source library provides a workaround, the paper itself should include a concise algorithmic description or pseudo-code for this critical transformation step.

comments.title: Hyperparameter tuning methodology limits experimental rigor
comments.quote: Since the interest was primarily to evaluate relative performance between different preprocessing methods, no significant attempt was made to train models to maximum performance or to venture beyond double descent, instead the primary tuning aspect was selecting an epoch depth beyond which additional did not have improved validation metrics.
comments.explanation: The authors state that only epoch depth was tuned, while other hyperparameters defaulted to fastai's initial learning rate finder and subsequent defaults. In comparative preprocessing studies, differing feature scales and augmentations often interact differently with learning rates, weight decay, batch sizes, and optimizer momentum. Restricting tuning to a single scalar (epochs) risks confounding preprocessing effects with suboptimal training configurations. This could explain why some advanced transformations (like noise injection) show performance degradation; the default schedule may simply be mismatched to noisier or expanded feature spaces. A more systematic tuning protocol or at least a discussion of why defaults are robust across all settings is needed to validate the relative comparisons.

comments.title: Overstated novelty claim for noise-based data augmentation
comments.quote: We believe the realization of a novel generalized solution to data augmentation by noise injection for tabular learning is a significant finding.
comments.explanation: The authors claim novelty for 'data augmentation by noise injection for tabular learning.' However, injecting Gaussian or Laplace noise into feature columns and duplicating samples is a well-established data augmentation technique in both tabular and deep learning literature (e.g., cited Perez & Wang, 2017; widely used in XGBoost/LightGBM pipelines via feature noise, and in early deep learning for tabular data). While Automunge packages it elegantly within its family-tree architecture, the core idea is not novel. The paper would benefit from tempering this claim and instead emphasizing the architectural integration, automated parameterization, or the specific empirical benefits observed in low-data regimes, rather than asserting the method itself is a novel solution.

comments.title: Textual summary conflicts with full-data experimental results
comments.quote: Data augmentation improved the results, sufficiently to draw the conclusion of material benefit for scenarios with underserved training data.
comments.explanation: The text claims that data augmentation improved results generally, but Table 8 shows that for the 'full data' scenario, the 'Retain w/ Augment' column yields an AUC of 0.8667 with a delta of (0.0003) relative to raw data, indicating a slight decrease rather than an improvement. The benefit is only pronounced in the 5% (0.0017) and 0.25% (0.0106) data subsets. The wording should be adjusted to clarify that the improvement is conditional on data scarcity, which aligns with the authors' actual premise but contradicts the broad 'improved the results' statement when taken out of context. This is a minor framing issue that risks misleading readers about the method's universal applicability.

zero_shot__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Zero Shot (deepseek-v4-flash)
zero_shot__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback: The paper provides a useful catalog of numeric encoding techniques implemented in the Automunge library, with some experimental validation on a Higgs dataset. However, several technical issues undermine its reliability: the MAD normalization formula and the log-normal transformation entry in Table 1 contain clear mathematical errors or ambiguous notation, and the naming of binning transformations in Table 4 is inconsistent. The claim of a novel generalized solution for tabular data augmentation by noise injection is overstated given the modest experimental gains and lack of comparison to existing augmentation methods. Overall, the paper needs correction of the formula errors and more rigorous justification of its central claims before acceptance.

comments.title: Incorrect MAD normalization formula
comments.explanation: The formula uses max in the numerator, which is inconsistent with the description 'scaled by median absolute deviation'. Standard practice for MAD normalization is (xi - median)/MAD. Using max shifts the center to the maximum rather than the median, and the resulting transformation does not achieve the intended robust scaling. This appears to be an author error rather than an OCR issue, as 'max' appears deliberately. The authors should clarify or correct the formula.

comments.title: Ambiguous log-normal transformation formula
comments.explanation: The formula is written with an exclamation mark that likely separates two alternative expressions, but the intended meaning is unclear. If the transformation is meant to apply a log transform followed by z-score, the second term should use ln(xi) rather than xi. The current notation is mathematically ambiguous and cannot be implemented as written. A clear, correct formula is needed.

comments.title: Inconsistent naming in binning table for binary encoding
comments.explanation: The naming pattern in Table 4 suggests that the three columns correspond to one-hot, ordinal, and binary encodings respectively. For the standard 'Powers of ten' row, the binary encoding is called 'pwbn'. For the negative-support variant, the binary encoding is listed as 'por3', which breaks the pattern (it would be expected to be 'pwb3' or similar). This is likely a typo or naming inconsistency that could cause confusion for users of the library. The authors should verify and correct the entry.

comments.title: Overclaimed novelty of noise injection for tabular augmentation
comments.quote: Experiments demonstrate the realization of a novel generalized solution to data augmentation by noise injection for tabular learning, as may materially benefit model performance in applications with underserved training data.
comments.explanation: The experimental results show only modest improvements (e.g., +0.0106 AUC on 0.25% data) and no comparison to other tabular augmentation methods such as SMOTE, Mixup, or feature-level noise injection from prior work. The claim of a 'novel generalized solution' is therefore not supported by the evidence presented. The paper would benefit from a more measured framing and a discussion of related augmentation techniques.

comments.title: Missing uncertainty quantification in experiment results
comments.quote: The AUC metrics shown are averages of repetition counts detailed in Appendix G.
comments.explanation: The paper reports only point estimates of AUC without standard deviations or confidence intervals, and the appendix detailing repetition counts is not provided in the submitted text. This makes it impossible to assess the statistical significance of the observed differences between methods. While this is a common omission, it weakens the conclusions, especially for the augmentation claims that rest on small improvements.

comments.title: Ambiguous parameter naming in noise injection tables
comments.explanation: The parameter name for flip probability appears with strikethrough formatting in both Tables 5 and 6, rendering it as 'fip ~~p~~rob'. It is likely intended to be 'fipprob' or 'fliprob', but the notation is unclear and could mislead readers trying to implement the method. The authors should use a clear, unambiguous label (e.g., 'flip_prob').

zero_shot__glm-4.7-flash.label: Zero Shot (glm-4.7-flash)
zero_shot__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: The paper presents a comprehensive survey and methodology for numeric feature preprocessing using the Automunge library, focusing on retention of zero points, noise injection for data augmentation, and complex family-tree transformations. The motivation for treating zero points seriously and applying noise injection to underrepresented data is sound. However, the review identified several critical formula errors in Table 1 (the 'lognorm' formula is unreadable and likely corrupted by OCR, and the 'MAD' formula incorrectly centers the subtraction on 'max' rather than a measure of central tendency) and a significant omission in Table 10 where integer transformation variants are listed without corresponding text descriptions.

comments.title: Incorrect MAD Normalization Formula
comments.explanation: While reading Table 1, I initially noticed the formula for the 'MAD' normalization is `(xi - max) / (MAD)`. Centering the data by subtracting the maximum value seems mathematically inconsistent with the purpose of a normalization that scales a feature to a distribution, as this would result in values that are always non-positive (or zero) and range from 0 to a negative number, effectively inverting the scale. Standard Median Absolute Deviation (MAD) normalizations typically center the data around the median or the mean (e.g., `(xi - median) / MAD`). I checked if this was a typographical error in the column layout, but given the context of 'centering and scaling', 'max' is an unlikely intended value. This is likely a typo for 'mean' or 'median' in the LaTeX source, which would be confusing for a reader attempting to implement this transformation.

comments.title: Unreadable Log-Normalization Formula
comments.explanation: I focused on the formula for the 'lognorm' normalization in Table 1. The LaTeX code appears corrupted by the OCR process. The snippet `ln(_xi_)_!_(_xi −µ_)_/σ_|` is indecipherable. The presence of the symbol `!_` suggests a missing parenthesis or a formatting artifact in the source PDF. Without a clear formula, it is impossible to understand how the transformation is intended to map data to a log-normal distribution scaled to Gaussian. This constitutes a major barrier to reproducing the library's methodology.

comments.title: Underspecified Integer Encoding Variants
comments.quote: Integer Sets|ntgr, ntg2, ntg3|to redundantly encode integer sets of unknown interpretation
comments.explanation: Table 10 summarizes use cases and lists 'ntgr, ntg2, ntg3' under the 'Integer Sets' category. I checked the text and Figure 4 caption ('ntgr' family trees) but found no definitions or descriptions for `ntg2` or `ntg3`. These symbols appear to be internal node names or specific transformation branches in the family tree structure, but presenting them in a high-level summary table without context creates ambiguity. While `ntgr` is defined in Figure 4 and Table 7, `ntg2` and `ntg3` are missing from the prose descriptions.
